data.title: U.S. Economy Best Served by Independent Federal Reserve, Fed's Kashkari Says
data.description:
By Jessica Coacci 




  The U.S. economy is best served by having an independent Federal Reserve that executes monetary-policy decisions based only on data and analysis, Minneapolis Fed President Neel Kashkari said in a virtual conversation with the Wisconsin Bankers Association. 



  With a new Fed chair on the horizon, and increased pressure on the committee after it received subpoenas from the Justice Department late last week relating to Chair Jerome Powell's testimony about renovations of the central bank's headquarters in Washington, Kashkari said Wednesday that the Fed's policy committee is focused on its economic goals as it deals with a complex scenario of a cooling labor market and inflation that has remained above its 2% target. 



  "We're doing our best to make the best judgments we can, but keeping politics out of it," he said. "I feel very confident that the committee will continue to make the best decisions we can, based on data and analysis, to achieve the dual mandate that Congress has charged us to try to achieve," he added. 



  Kashkari will rotate in as a voting member of the Federal Open Market Committee this year when officials decide on where to set interest rates at the end of January after three consecutive quarter-point cuts through the end of December. Ahead of the meeting, Kashkari said any future rate decisions would depend on incoming data and which side of the central bank's dual mandate poses a bigger risk to the economy. 



  "We have a labor market that is showing some signs of weakness," he said. "Inflation is still too high, but it is trending, at least, in the right direction," he added. 



  Last week, the Labor Department reported that the unemployment rate ticked down to 4.4%, although it crept higher in 2025. 



  On CNBC last week, Kashkari said rates were pretty close to neutral right now--a level that doesn't slow down or stimulate the economy. He also expressed skepticism toward recent inflation data that showed consumer prices rose 2.7% in November from a year earlier, citing issues with the data collection from the prolonged government shutdown that lasted until mid November. 



  "It's going to take a few more months until the data is fully clean again," he said Wednesday. 





  Write to Jessica Coacci at jessica.coacci@wsj.com 





  (END) Dow Jones Newswires



  January 14, 2026 14:37 ET (19:37 GMT)


Copyright (c) 2026 Dow Jones & Company, Inc.

data.title: How Trump's affordability push is prompting Wall Street to rethink what's next for tariffs
data.description:
By Isabel Wang and Joy Wiltermuth

The fate of President Trump's tariffs on foreign imports is being closely watched on Wall Street.

As investors anxiously wait for the U.S. Supreme Court to issue a ruling on the bulk of President Trump's tariffs, some on Wall Street have started to question whether high tariff levels fit with the White House's new affordability push.

Trump previously said any Supreme Court ruling against the legality of his tariffs would be "a complete mess" to deal with. Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent recently defended the tariffs, and provided an outline on how any missing tariff revenue could be replaced if the nation's highest court determines Trump misused emergency powers to increase levies against U.S. trade partners.

A ruling expected on Wednesday morning didn't materialize, following a similar disappointment last Friday.

Read: The Supreme Court may rule Wednesday on Trump's tariffs. But here's what it means if it holds off again.

Yet after previously calling affordability a "hoax," the Trump administration kicked off 2026 with a series of initiatives aimed at the hot-button issue for many households. So far, these relate to mortgage rates and the affordability crisis in housing, as well as the idea of a one-year 10% cap on the interest charged on credit cards.

That has prompted some to wonder whether the administration might view a Supreme Court ruling against the tariffs as an opportunity to dial back the levies.

"There's an affordability concern," said Jeffrey Schulze, head of economic and market strategy at ClearBridge Investments. "One of the easiest levers the administration can pull is to reduce tariffs this year until we can get to the midterm elections."

While Schulze expects the administration to look to replace any tariffs deemed illegal, he isn't convinced there will be "a sense of urgency to do that in a very fast capacity." Any replacement tariffs "also may come in at a more moderate rate than we have currently," he added.

The White House didn't respond to a MarketWatch request for comment.

How much tariff revenue is at stake for markets?

The Supreme Court still is expected to provide clarity on Trump's use of the International Emergency Economic Powers Act (IEEPA) of 1977 last year to quickly issue sweeping tariffs against major trade partners.

The amount of IEEPA tariffs already collected were estimated at $133.5 billion, according to Bernard Yaros, U.S. lead economist at Oxford Economics, using U.S. Customs and Border Protection data through the end of December.

"Investors don't like uncertainty - and right now that includes both the timing of the Supreme Court's ruling and the substance of the decision," said Bret Kenwell, U.S. investment analyst at eToro.

But should the court rule "against the current tariff policy, it could introduce a new set of complications, reopening key questions around trade policy, enforcement and what happens to all of the tariff income collected," he noted.

However, tariff concerns are now in the rearview mirror for many market participants, as President Trump has pivoted to the next phase of his policy agenda on making life for American consumers more affordable, particularly with this fall's midterm elections approaching, said David Russell, vice president of market intelligence at TradeStation.

"The market looks out and sees the tariffs are very much a 2025 story. They do have lingering effects, but this ship may have sailed in this story - whether it's the potential Supreme Court ruling or something else that basically pushes it into the rearview mirror," Russell told MarketWatch in a phone interview on Wednesday.

U.S. stocks were lower on Wednesday afternoon, with technology names under pressure after China reportedly banned cybersecurity software from the U.S. The S&P 500 SPX was falling nearly 1% in afternoon action, while the Nasdaq Composite COMP was tumbling 1.5% and the Dow Jones Industrial Average DJIA was off 0.5%, according to FactSet data.

Investors in the bond market have been reluctant to make big moves for weeks, in part because tariff revenue has been viewed as a slight offset to the nearly $2 trillion U.S. deficit. The yield on the 10-year Treasury note BX:TMUBMUSD10Y were falling 3 basis points Wednesday to trade near 4.14%, while the 30-year rate BX:TMUBMUSD30Y was off 4 basis points at 4.79%, according to FactSet.

Changes wouldn't come quickly - even if the Supreme Court curbs tariffs

Morgan Stanley analysts Ariana Salvatore and Bradley Tian said in a Jan. 7 client note that they expect U.S. trade policy to remain restrictive, but that any Supreme Court ruling against the Trump administration could give the White House "scope" to take a "lighter-touch approach" to its tariff regime "given a recent political focus on affordability."

Still, any process to apply tariffs outside of emergency powers ends up being "pretty slow to start with," said Amar Reganti, fixed-income strategist at Hartford Funds.

In some cases, it can require a fairly thorough study from the Commerce Department, he noted, depending on which clauses are being used. Any replacement tariffs also could require interagency coordination, which would also take time.

"It's not as fast and rapid of the IEEPA tariffs are," Reganti said. "It will be slower."

Related: What other tariffs could Trump roll back as economic worries persist?

Gordon Gottsegen contributed.

-Isabel Wang -Joy Wiltermuth

This content was created by MarketWatch, which is operated by Dow Jones & Co. MarketWatch is published independently from Dow Jones Newswires and The Wall Street Journal. 




  (END) Dow Jones Newswires



  01-14-26 1427ET


Copyright (c) 2026 Dow Jones & Company, Inc.

data.title: The financial elite aren't worried about a stock-market bubble yet - but you can't afford to be so complacent
data.description:
By Mark Hulbert

Investors know bull markets end, of course. But even the global leaders descending on Davos may underestimate the dangers.

Even the global elite may not be eyeing the appropriate risks in 2026.

Long-term investment success requires us to be fearful when the majority is complacent. This is one of those times.

It's not just retail investors who are complacent these days. Complacency is widespread among sophisticated institutional investors who should know better, too.

Particularly noteworthy is the attitude of the global elite who are gathering in Davos, Switzerland, next week for the annual World Economic Forum. The WEF was created in large part to anticipate global risks, and yet this year's participants seem largely oblivious to the possibility that equities in general, and U.S. equities in particular, are forming a bubble that could soon burst.

In the 2025 edition of the WEF's annual Global Risks Report (the 2026 report is due soon), participants placed "asset bubble burst" in 19th place in a ranking of global risks deemed "most likely to present a material crisis on a global scale" last year.

You might excuse this complacency on the grounds that the elite are focusing on global, existential questions - issues such as war, extreme weather events, the breakdown of civil society and so forth. But that would be crediting them with too much unselfishness, given their behavior the last time the global financial markets nearly became unraveled.

That occurred in the global financial crisis, of course. In the January 2009 issue of the WEF's Global Risks Report - two months before the crisis finally bottomed, participants ranked "asset price collapse" as the biggest near-term risk the world then faced. Surely we can all agree that the risk of a bubble in early 2009 was far less than it is today.

If you had any doubt, consider the S&P 500's SPX valuation in early 2009 versus now. As the table below shows, the S&P 500 then was much more reasonably valued than it is now.

Recency bias

'There's a difference ... between being told that markets can fall by more than 50% and having it burned into your memory.'

The world elite's preoccupation in 2009 with a collapse in asset prices - and its complacency today - reveals what's known as recency bias, which is defined as "a cognitive bias that favors recent events over historic ones. ... [R]ecency bias gives greater importance to the most recent event."

Read: The Fed's failures set the stage for the Trump-Powell turmoil. Here's one solution.

There is no easy antidote to recency bias. Becoming a student of financial-market history is helpful, though probably not enough to overcome recency bias. More important is having lived through a bursting bubble, or at least suffering through a severe and protracted bear market. Edward McQuarrie, a professor emeritus at Santa Clara University, and William Bernstein of Efficient Frontiers have written that "there's a difference ... between being told that markets can fall by more than 50% and having it burned into your memory banks by seeing your net worth halved in real time as the economy careens toward the precipice."

One possible solution is to enlist the services of a financial planner who has been involved in the markets long enough to have experienced a severe bear market. When this bull market does finally come to an end, you will be glad to have that knowledge on your side.

Mark Hulbert is a regular contributor to MarketWatch. His Hulbert Ratings tracks investment newsletters that pay a flat fee to be audited. He can be reached at mark@hulbertratings.com

More: Inflation fighter? Risk hedge? Why gold investors shouldn't believe all they hear.

Also read: These 20 cheap S&P 500 stocks can cushion the blow in a market downturn

-Mark Hulbert

This content was created by MarketWatch, which is operated by Dow Jones & Co. MarketWatch is published independently from Dow Jones Newswires and The Wall Street Journal. 




  (END) Dow Jones Newswires



  01-14-26 1432ET


Copyright (c) 2026 Dow Jones & Company, Inc.

data.title: Leap Showcases Hybrid Roofing Workflows at The 2026 International Roofing Expo
data.description:
Leap Showcases Hybrid Roofing Workflows at The 2026 International Roofing Expo

Leap Showcases Hybrid Roofing Workflows at The 2026 International Roofing Expo

PR Newswire

BALTIMORE, Jan. 14, 2026

BALTIMORE, Jan. 14, 2026 /PRNewswire/ -- Leap, a leading software platform built to help roofers and remodelers win and manage more jobs, is heading to the 2026 International Roofing Expo (IRE) in Las Vegas, January 20–22. Contractors visiting booth #1745 can see live demos, meet the Leap team, and enter to win exclusive giveaways.

As roofing businesses grow more complex, contractors are being asked to move faster, manage tighter margins, and deliver a more professional experience without adding unnecessary overhead. At IRE 2026, Leap is showing how its platform simplifies the workflow from the first conversation to the final invoice, so contractors can sell confidently and keep jobs running smoothly.

Built for How Roofing Actually Works Today

Leap highlights how many roofing businesses operate in reality: managing a mix of retail and insurance work on one connected solution. Rather than forcing separate systems or workarounds, Leap supports selling, reporting, production, and visibility in a single workflow.

At Leap's booth, contractors can:

  • Walk through real sales and production workflows
  • See how retail and insurance jobs stay connected without added complexity
  • Talk with the Leap team about reducing handoffs, errors, and downstream surprises
  • Explore how process improvements translate into real business impact
  • Enter to win IRE-only giveaways and grab new Leap swag

"No sales pressure. No generic demos," said Patrick Fingles, CEO at Leap. "Contractors want tools that work the way they do, not software that creates more friction. That's what we're showing at IRE."

One Solution. Fewer Gaps.

Leap helps contractors simplify their workflow with software that works as hard as they do. With Leap SalesPro and Leap CRM, roofing teams can sell accurately, protect margins, hand jobs cleanly into production, and gain reporting to run the business with confidence—whether a job starts as retail or insurance.

Visit Leap at IRE 2026

Contractors can find Leap at booth #1745 for hands-on demos, real conversations, and a closer look at how modern roofing businesses keep sales and production aligned. If you're evaluating how to simplify operations in 2026, Leap is worth your time on the show floor.

About Leap

Leap makes it easier for roofers and remodelers to win and manage more jobs. Built for real-world contracting, Leap simplifies estimating, sales, communication, project management, and reporting to help contractors save time, protect margins, and run stronger businesses.

Media Contact:
Natalie Rockefeller
Sr. Director of Marketing
nrockefeller@leaptodigital.com

data.title: 5Star Life Insurance Company Appoints Ronald R. Gendreau Chair of the Board
data.description:
5Star Life Insurance Company Appoints Ronald R. Gendreau Chair of the Board

Gendreau’s extensive expertise to contribute to 5Star Life’s strategic direction

5Star Life Insurance Company (5Star Life) announced today the appointment of Ronald R. Gendreau as Chair of its board of directors, effective January 1, 2026.

This press release features multimedia. View the full release here: https://www.businesswire.com/news/home/20260113813760/en/

Ronald Gendreau

“We are pleased to elevate Ron to the role of Chair of 5Star Life board of directors. His combination of deep industry experience and intimate knowledge of 5Star Life will guide our strategic efforts as we position 5Star Life for future growth,” said General Larry O. Spencer, USAF (Ret.), Chairman and President of 5Star Life’s affiliate, Armed Forces Benefit Association, and President of 5Star Life.

Ron began his insurance career as an actuary in 1976. He served in leadership roles at Travelers Insurance, Unum, Colonial Life, and The Hartford for 40 years, gaining experience in the Individual, Worksite and Group Life Insurance markets. He joined the 5Star Life Board in 2013 and has served as Lead Independent Director since 2018.

"I am honored to take on this new role and look forward to collaborating with the leadership team to advance 5Star Life's mission and drive innovation across its product offerings," stated Ron Gendreau.

About 5Star Life

5Star Life Insurance Company (5Star Life) is the primary underwriter of the Armed Forces Benefit Association (AFBA) member policies. In addition, it extends the enterprise’s mission to civilians serving our country in the workplace. As a growing provider of group and worksite voluntary products, 5Star Life serves a diverse clientele as an insurance provider to individuals and organizations and as a trusted advisor to brokers. Headquartered in Alexandria, VA, 5Star Life is currently licensed in 49 states, the District of Columbia, Puerto Rico, the U.S. Virgin Islands, Guam, and American Samoa.

About Armed Forces Benefit Association

With the support of the General of the Army, Dwight D. Eisenhower, the Armed Forces Benefit Association (AFBA) was established in 1947 in the basement of the Pentagon to ease the strain on military members and their families who, at the time, could not purchase life insurance that would pay a death benefit if the member was killed in combat. Today, headquartered in Alexandria, VA, AFBA continues to honor its mission, in war and peace, promoting the welfare of its members by providing survivor and other benefits to those who serve this great nation, including members of the uniformed services, first responders, government employees, and their families. AFBA has 915,000 members with $57 billion in death benefits in force and has paid $2 billion of death benefits since inception. AFBA death benefits are primarily underwritten by its affiliate, 5Star Life Insurance Company (a Lincoln, Nebraska domiciled company). AFBA is not affiliated with any government agency.

Raquel Fuentes
202-441-0794
rfuentes@afba.com

View source version on businesswire.com: https://www.businesswire.com/news/home/20260113813760/en/

data.title: Generational Wealth™ Launches, Nears $1 Billion Post–Merger and Acquisition
data.description:
Generational Wealth™ Launches, Nears $1 Billion Post–Merger and Acquisition

Generational Wealth™, a newly launched wealth management and financial planning firm, announced a merger under their unified brand following a joint $100mm acquisition. Following the transaction, the firm enters the market with over $900 million in Assets Under Management and Administration. *

“This launch is a deliberate step toward reshaping what wealth management firms and financial planning should be,” said Branden Schiralli, CPWA®, CEPA®, President of Generational Wealth™. “In our view much of the industry remains siloed and product driven, we continue to set ourselves apart by challenging the status quo and building a new kind of wealth firm — one that isn’t constrained by outdated models or backward incentives that places firms’ interests ahead of clients. The industry didn’t need another firm - it needed a better one.”

At the core of its planning is SIMPLE™, the firm’s proprietary framework, which is a step-by step financial advisory process — encompassing foundational financial planning, investment implementation, exit planning, and wealth transition. Generational Wealth’s accomplished in-house investment team constructs active, risk-aligned portfolios designed to adapt to both client goals as well as market cycles. “We’re not your typical wealth management firm — that’s intentional,” added Schiralli. “Committed to our fiduciary standard, we believe financial planning should feel personal, not packaged. Clients’ assets should be managed in-house by our investment team, not handed off. And advice should be thoughtful, not templated.”

With assets over $900 million in Assets Under Management and Administration*, the firm is scaling with intention by investing in infrastructure, technology, and talent to better serve clients — helping them create not just wealth, but Generational Wealth for themselves and their heirs for generations to come.

About Generational Wealth™

Generational Wealth™ is a cutting edge, revolutionary financial planning and investment management firm headquartered in North Palm Beach, Florida. With over 200 years of combined experience, the enterprise is built on the mission of Generating Wealth for Generations by helping clients combine fiduciary financial planning, in-house portfolio design, and creating strategies to help Generate, Protect & Transfer Wealth.

https://www.my-generationalwealth.com

For more information: gw@my-wealth.com

data.title: The wait for a tariff ruling could signal a Trump win - or a refund headache
data.description:
By Victor Reklaitis

Supreme Court justices may be taking time to address the issue of tariff refunds

The much-awaited ruling on President Donald Trump's tariffs was not among the opinions issued on Wednesday by the Supreme Court.

The likelihood of the Supreme Court ruling in favor of President Donald Trump in a closely watched tariff case increased moderately Wednesday after the court again held off on releasing its decision.

The high court has indicated it is acting in an expedited manner in this case, which has massive economic stakes, but the justices don't typically give hints about exactly when a particular opinion will be issued.

Investors, importers and others who had braced for a ruling on Wednesday morning were left disappointed, just as they were last Friday.

Some analysts have said it's a good omen for Trump if the Supreme Court keeps holding off on releasing its decision. It's possible that there is significant debate among the justices, rather than a widespread view that lower courts were right to rule in favor of the importers challenging a wide swath of Trump's tariffs.

"We agree that a long wait for the tariff decision is probably a better sign for the Trump administration than it is for importers, but it is no guarantee of the outcome," said Kelsey Christensen, a trade attorney at law firm Clark Hill, in an email to MarketWatch on Wednesday. She added that court watchers are "not in long-wait territory yet," given the court's track record and the fact that it heard oral arguments in the case just two months ago.

"November to January is lightning speed for the court to hear and publish a major opinion," Christensen said. "As the wait continues, we suspect that the justices are navigating and fine-tuning a majority opinion in addition to one or more dissents or concurrences."

One betting market, Kalshi, recently was putting the likelihood of a Trump win in the tariff case at 35%, up from as low as 22% just before the Supreme Court issued its opinions for the day without releasing one that addressed the president's import taxes. Polymarket was putting Trump's chance for a victory at 33%, up from a low around 21% on Wednesday morning.

It's possible Chief Justice John Roberts and his colleagues are taking time to address the issue of tariff refunds, according to Terence Lau, dean of Syracuse University's law school and a former trade attorney for Ford Motor Co. (F). Analysts have been anticipating scenarios in which the court rules against Trump's tariffs and calls for big refunds to importers, along with outcomes in which refunds aren't required.

"The longer wait suggests the justices are debating the scope of the remedy," Lau told MarketWatch. "For example, they may be trying to find a middle ground, perhaps invalidating the tariffs but limiting refunds to 'prospective relief' only, not to past tariffs collected. The longer the tariffs remain in place, the more catastrophic a retroactive refund becomes for the U.S. Treasury."

Lau also said he's expecting multiple opinions in this case, with potentially "a very bare majority opinion with some justices only joining some parts of the majority."

The Supreme Court, which has a 6-3 conservative majority, is weighing in after lower courts last year found that Trump exceeded his authority in using the International Emergency Economic Powers Act of 1977 to justify his many country-specific tariffs. Opponents of Trump's IEEPA-based tariffs have emphasized that the law doesn't explicitly mention tariffs in its text, and that no president has ever used IEEPA to impose tariffs.

Trump has defended his IEEPA tariffs repeatedly and claimed that the U.S. economy would be hurt significantly if the Supreme Court were to strike them down. He reiterated that stance in a social-media post on Monday, while voicing concerns about the potential for tariff refunds.

"It would be a complete mess, and almost impossible for our Country to pay," Trump said in part.

Analysts have said the issue of refunds is crucial for the bond market BX:TMUBMUSD10Y, noting that rating agency S&P maintained its rating for U.S. sovereign debt with a stable outlook in part because of incoming tariff revenue.

Trump administration officials have indicated they're ready to maintain their tariffs through other avenues, such as by invoking authorities granted in sections 301 and 122 of the Trade Act of 1974, as well as Section 232 of the Trade Expansion Act of 1962.

-Victor Reklaitis

This content was created by MarketWatch, which is operated by Dow Jones & Co. MarketWatch is published independently from Dow Jones Newswires and The Wall Street Journal. 




  (END) Dow Jones Newswires



  01-14-26 1409ET


Copyright (c) 2026 Dow Jones & Company, Inc.

data.title: Trustpoint Xposure Launches as The Only AEO-Certified PR Agency Aligning PR with Answer Engines
data.description:
Trustpoint Xposure today announced its official launch as the only AEO-certified PR agency built to align public relations directly with answer engines, responding to the rapid shift from traditional search results to AI-generated answers.

POST FALLS, IDAHO / ACCESS Newswire / January 14, 2026 / As AI assistants increasingly determine which brands are surfaced, referenced, and trusted, visibility now depends on more than exposure. Trustpoint Xposure was created to bridge public relations and AEO, ensuring that brand narratives are not only published but also understood and validated by AI systems.

Aligning PR With How Answer Engines Work

Answer engines prioritize clarity, consistency, and credibility. Trustpoint Xposure structures PR campaigns so that brand information can be clearly interpreted and confidently cited by AI search systems.

Rather than treating PR and optimization as separate disciplines, the agency aligns messaging, structured content, and authoritative signals into a unified framework. This alignment allows brands to strengthen their long-term online authority as AI reshapes discovery.

Media Placement as a Verification Signal

In an AI-first environment, media placement plays a different role than in traditional PR. Trustpoint Xposure treats earned coverage as a verification layer that reinforces brand facts across trusted sources, helping search engines assess legitimacy and relevance.

Each placement is selected and structured to support a consistent brand footprint, rather than short-term exposure alone.

Reputation and Consistency in AI Search

Trustpoint Xposure also integrates reputation management into its PR model to ensure brand signals remain aligned across owned, earned, and third-party platforms. Inconsistent or conflicting information can weaken how AI systems evaluate trust.

By maintaining consistency across the digital ecosystem, the agency helps brands build durable authority that AI systems can rely on over time.

Addressing the Core AI Search Question

As more organizations ask how to rank first in AI search, Trustpoint Xposure focuses on fundamentals rather than tactics. The agency's approach centers on making brands legible to machines through structured PR, authoritative validation, and clear entity positioning.

About Trustpoint Xposure

Trustpoint Xposure is an AEO-certified PR agency designed to align public relations with answer engines. The agency helps brands earn visibility and citations in AI search results through a combination of AEO-driven strategy, authoritative media placement, and reputation-focused PR systems.

Jack Smith
Media Manager
Trustpoint Xposure
contact@trustpointxposure.com

SOURCE: Trustpoint Xposure



View the original press release on ACCESS Newswire

data.title: Citigroup's stock turns lower after a rare earnings miss. Russia is one reason.
data.description:
By Tomi Kilgore

The bank booked a billion-dollar-plus loss related to its plan to divest assets in Russia

Citigroup's fell after the bank reported a rare earnings miss, due to losses related to asset sales in Russia.

Shares of Citigroup reversed into negative territory Wednesday, after the money-center bank missed fourth-quarter earnings expectations, due primarily to a large loss related to its plan to sell assets in Russia.

Chief Executive Jane Fraser said on the post earnings call with analysts that Citi's banking business had a "record year," and that the outlook for the economy made her upbeat about 2026. She said a favorable interest rate environment, the strength of the consumer, and the amount of money being spent by technology companies, the economy is poised to keep growing.

"With inflation now at normal levels globally, almost every central bank is becoming more accommodating," Fraser said, according to a FactSet transcript. "And while the labor market in the US has softened, capital investment remains strong, especially in tech."

The stock (C) had initially rallied after the results were reported, because if the one-time Russia loss was excluded profit would have topped expectations, but sentiment seemed to succumb to weakness in shares of its peers and the broader stock market.

Net income for Citigroup's fourth quarter dropped 13.5% from the same period a year ago to $2.47 billion, and earnings per share declined to $1.19 from $1.34. The average analyst EPS estimate compiled by FactSet was $1.65.

That miss snapped a seven quarter streak of beats, and was just the second bottom-line miss in at least the past five years, based on available FactSet data back to January 2021.

But Citi said the results included a previously disclosed loss of $1.2 billion, due to the held-for-sale accounting treatment related to the bank's plan to sell AO Citibank in Russia. Excluding that notable item, EPS would have been $1.81.

Total revenue grew 2.1% to $19.87 billion, boosted by growth in all of its business segments, and would have been $21 billion if the notable item had been excluded. The FactSet revenue consensus was $20.95 billion.

The stock slumped 3.7% in recent afternoon trading, reversing a premarket gain of as much as 2% seen soon after the results were released.

1-minute chart, as of 2 p.m. Eastern

Citi's earnings followed reports from fellow banking giants Bank of America (BAC), which beat expectations, and Wells Fargo (WFC), which missed, and on Tuesday by sector leader JPMorgan Chase (JPM), which also disappointed investors. BofA shares dropped 3.9% despite the beat, while Wells Fargo shares slid 4.7%. And the S&P 500 index SPX gave up 0.9%.

Return on tangible common equity for the fourth quarter, a key measure of profitability, fell to 5.1% from 6.1%, to miss the FactSet consensus of 6%, but RoTCE would have increased to 6.8% excluding the Russia loss.

For 2025, net RoTCE was 7.7%, and Chief Executive Jane Fraser said the bank was committed to reaching its goal of RoTCE of 10% to 11% in 2026.

Citi said provisions for credit losses in the latest quarter improved to $2.22 billion from $2.59 billion, amid higher lending activity and changes in credit quality.

Within Citi's markets business, fixed-income revenue slipped 1% to $3.5 billion but topped the FactSet consensus of $3.34 billion, while equity-markets revenue declined 1% to $1.1 billion to miss expectations of $1.2 billion.

Citi's stock has pulled back 9.2% since Jan. 5, when it closed at its highest price since Nov. 5, 2008, but has still rallied 52.3% over the past 12 months to outperform its peers.

-Tomi Kilgore

This content was created by MarketWatch, which is operated by Dow Jones & Co. MarketWatch is published independently from Dow Jones Newswires and The Wall Street Journal. 




  (END) Dow Jones Newswires



  01-14-26 1400ET


Copyright (c) 2026 Dow Jones & Company, Inc.
